title: Hillary's Locker Cabinet with Charging Cubbies
summary: A face-framed locker cabinet with a bank of build-to-fit drawers and a charging cubby wired through a drilled back panel.
description:
This is a real face-framed cabinet, which means every joint is reinforced by the frame itself rather than relying on the box joints alone — get the box built square from the start and the drawers will slide the way they're supposed to. The drawer faces are sized off your actual 1x8 boards rather than a fixed number: measure your boards' real width, add 1/4" for drawer-side allowance, and cut the cubby openings to that number. Lumber runs a little different width from batch to batch, and building to your own material instead of a spec sheet is what makes drawers that fit.

The drawers themselves are cut-to-fit, not cut-to-spec — build each one slightly oversized, then trim it down to its own cubby opening (about 1/8" clearance per side for a first attempt, tighter if you're confident in your cuts), because no two openings in a hand-built cabinet are perfectly identical. This is also a charging station: one drawer gets a hole drilled through the back panel to route a cable through. Because this is a tall, top-heavy cabinet, it must be secured to a wall stud, not left freestanding — that's not optional trim, it's what keeps it from tipping.

tools.note: Alternative to countersunk screws or nails for the carcass joints.
Drills the cable pass-through hole in the back panel for the charging cubby.

materials.name: Wall anchors or lag screws (to secure to a stud)
Candle wax
materials.note: Rubbed on drawer bottoms after finishing for a smooth slide.

cutList.note: Splits the cabinet into the locker side and the drawer cubby side.
Measure your 1x8 boards' actual width and cut these to that width plus 1/4" for drawer allowance.
Cut to fit — treat this as a starting size and trim each drawer to its own opening.
Height matches your actual 1x8 board width — cut to fit each opening.

steps.title: Build the box square
steps.body:
If you get this box built square and right, your drawers will slide great — take your time, mark all your joints, and check for square at least twice. Measure the actual width of your 1x8 boards to get your drawer face heights, add 1/4" to that number, and cut your cubby dividers to that width for well-fitting drawers.

If you don't have a pocket-hole jig, add the main divider (next step) before assembling the box — you won't be able to fit a drill in there afterward.

steps.title: Add the main divider
steps.body: Attach the main locker divider with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws and glue, or with countersunk screws or nails and glue.

steps.title: Add sides, top, and bottom shelf
steps.body: Add the sides, top, and bottom shelf using the pocket-hole jig or just screws or nails and glue — the whole box is face framed, so every joint is reinforced.

steps.title: Attach the back
steps.body: Attach the back panel to all the fixed shelves with glue and 1-1/4" finish nails.

steps.title: Attach the face frame
steps.body: Attach the face frame stiles and rails to the front of the cabinet, keeping every joint flush and square.

steps.title: Add top and bottom trim
steps.body: Add the top and bottom trim to the cabinet.

steps.title: Add the crown
steps.body: Add the crown trim to finish the top of the cabinet.

steps.title: Build the drawers
steps.body:
The trick with these drawers is building each one to fit its own opening. For a first attempt, leave about 1/8" clearance on each side; if you're confident in your cuts, 1/16" gives a tighter fit. Tack the plywood bottom onto each drawer box. For the charging drawer, drill a hole through the back panel to route a cable through.

Each drawer opening may be slightly different, so build and fit each drawer to its own cubby rather than to a single measurement. Rub candle wax on the drawer bottoms after finishing for the smoothest slide.

steps.title: Install the drawers and faces
steps.body: Slide each drawer box into its cubby. Cut the drawer faces to fit and position them over the drawer boxes, then nail on and add the knobs.

steps.title: Add hooks and secure to the wall
steps.body: Add the coat hooks. This cabinet is tall and top-heavy — secure it to a wall stud behind it with wall anchors or lag screws to prevent tipping before it's used.
materials: Wall anchors or lag screws (to secure to a stud)

images.alt: Locker cabinet with charging cubbies and build-to-fit drawers
